joke.raw_joke: My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding... — She got mad and said she's never playing scrabble with me again

blocks.content:
### seed-226

**MILO_OFFICIAL_SCRIPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-226
edit_level: polish

script:
- setup: "My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding."
- punchline: "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again."
- tag: ~

changes_made: Capitalized "Scrabble" (proper noun). Dropped "She got mad and said" — "She got mad" is implied and "said" is unnecessary padding before the punchline. Starting the punchline directly with "She said she's never playing Scrabble..." is tighter. Removed the ellipsis from the setup — it was doing the right work rhythmically but the natural pause after a period serves the same function without telegraphing the beat.

caption_notes: Two cards. Setup: "My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding." Punchline: "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again." — The pivot from apparent infidelity to Scrabble is the entire joke; the punchline caption must stand alone on its own card so the audience lands on "Scrabble" as the reveal.

timing_notes: Setup delivered with mild sheepishness — a man confessing something. The word "cheating" should sit in the audience's mind for the full setup duration. 0.4s pause — this is where the audience is still processing "letters" in the infidelity register. Punchline delivered flat, as if the narrator is still unclear why this was a problem. He was holding Scrabble letters. He sees no issue.

tts_notes: "Scrabble" — two syllables, SCRAB-bul. Standard pronunciation, no issues. Confirm TTS doesn't swallow the "b."

quality_flag: strong

---

### seed-226

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Husband / Narrator | Jerry Gold — Classic Dad persona | Jerry is absolutely the man hiding Scrabble tiles for strategic advantage. His "authority with blind spots" archetype delivers the setup with mild sheepishness (correctly — he knows "cheating on her" sounds bad) and then delivers the punchline with the energy of someone who still doesn't entirely understand why his wife is upset. He was holding extra S-tiles. He sees no moral issue. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Jerry: Casual home wardrobe — no teal jacket, just a shirt. He may be holding a small cloth bag (a Scrabble tile pouch) or a fistful of Scrabble tiles — the letters he was hiding. The prop makes the visual pivot explicit: these are the letters. They are Scrabble tiles. His wife was wrong about what she found.
- The wife is not cast — her line ("she's never playing Scrabble with me again") is reported speech and Jerry delivers it to camera. The wife's absence keeps the camera on Jerry's face, which is the correct comedic register: a man reporting a consequence he still doesn't fully accept as justified.
- A Scrabble board partially visible on a table behind him (mid-game, abandoned) would silently confirm the context without additional cast.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Jerry to camera, mild sheepishness building — he knows the word "cheating" is in the sentence and he knows how it lands. His expression is a man who is being honest about something that doesn't look great but is, in his view, completely defensible. The Scrabble tiles or bag are in his hand or visible nearby.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Jerry. He delivers "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again" with the flat tone of someone reporting an overreaction. His expression is: mild incredulity. He had tiles. He was keeping them. This is strategy. The Scrabble tiles in his hand or on the table confirm the reveal. He is still holding them.

### seed-226

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-226
concepts_count: 2

Milo script: ✅ Received. Setup: "My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding." Punchline: "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again." "Cheating" sits in the audience's mind through the full setup. Scrabble reveal is the pivot.
Riley casting honored: Jerry Gold (Classic Dad) solo. Wife not cast — reported speech only. Jerry may be holding Scrabble tiles or a tile bag. Scrabble board partially visible on a table behind him. He's still holding the tiles.

---

**Concept A — "The Confession"**
Jerry at a kitchen or dining table, Scrabble board behind him mid-game. He's holding tiles. He delivers the story with mild sheepishness that never quite becomes guilt.
Duration: 8.8s — Two-beat solo. The Scrabble board visible before the punchline lets the audience solve it early if they're sharp, or get surprised if they're not.

Master video prompt:
"Suburban dining room or kitchen table area, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm overhead lighting, vertical 9:16 portrait. Jerry Gold (middle-aged, expressive eyebrows, handlebar mustache, casual shirt — no teal jacket) standing or seated at a table. On the table behind or beside him: a Scrabble board mid-game — wooden tile rack with letter tiles, the game board with words partially formed, scattered tiles. Jerry holds a few Scrabble letter tiles in one hand — small wooden squares with black letters on them. His other hand is free for gestures. His expression is mild sheepishness that never quite commits to remorse. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — THE MAN WITH SOMETHING TO SAY
A suburban dining room — warm overhead light, a wooden table. At the table: Jerry Gold — middle-aged man, expressive eyebrows at their natural height, handlebar mustache, wearing a casual shirt (no teal jacket — this is at-home Jerry). Behind him on the table surface, partially visible: a Scrabble board mid-game. The board has words formed in crossed patterns, wooden tile racks at each side, scattered tiles. The game appears to have been interrupted — one rack has tiles face-up, the other is empty. Jerry faces the viewer. In one hand, he holds several Scrabble letter tiles — small wooden squares, black letters printed on them, clutched in his palm. His expression is the opening frame of a confession: slightly guarded, mildly sheepish, the face of a man who knows what he's about to say will sound bad and is deciding to say it anyway. He is not deeply worried. He is a man with a story.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.2s): SETUP
Same dining room. Jerry Gold (mustache, casual shirt, Scrabble tiles in hand) faces the viewer. His expression is measured sheepishness — he is delivering the word "cheating" and he knows how it sounds. One hand holds the clutch of Scrabble tiles; the other makes a small conciliatory gesture. He is confessing something. The word "cheating" sits in the air. The word "letters" follows. The audience is building a picture of infidelity. Behind Jerry, the Scrabble board on the table is visible but not the focal point — it's background information that attentive viewers will register and most will not, until the punchline. The tiles in his hand are visible but could be anything at this distance.
Caption: "My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding."

Scene 3 (5.2–5.6s): BEAT
Same dining room, closer framing on Jerry. He pauses. His sheepishness shifts — it doesn't deepen into guilt, it settles into mild incredulity. He is about to report his wife's reaction. He doesn't entirely agree with it.

Scene 4 (5.6–8.8s): PUNCHLINE — THE GAME
Same dining room. Jerry Gold (mustache, casual shirt) faces the viewer. He delivers his wife's verdict with the flat tone of someone reporting an overreaction to a legitimate strategy. His expression is mild incredulity — he had tiles, he was keeping them, this is how you win. He lifts the hand holding the Scrabble tiles slightly — the small wooden squares now clearly visible, black letters facing out. These are the letters. These are what he was hiding. They are Scrabble tiles. Behind him, the abandoned Scrabble board on the table is now legible in context — the game is over, one side has left, the board sits mid-word unfinished. He is still holding the tiles. He has not put them back. He sees no reason to put them back. They are his tiles. He earned them. He was cheating, yes, but at Scrabble. He does not understand why this distinction isn't helping his case.
Caption: "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s silent | 2.0–5.2s setup | 5.6–8.8s punchline. "Scrabble" capitalized on punchline card.
Voice: Jerry mild sheepishness for setup — he knows "cheating" sounds bad. 0.4s pause. Punchline flat, delivered as reported speech — his wife's verdict, quoted with mild incredulity. He does not agree with the severity. He also has not returned the tiles.
Risk: LOW. Single character, domestic setting. Scrabble tiles are small but distinctive — wooden squares with letters. The board in the background is context-setting. If AI gen can't render Scrabble tiles clearly, any small square objects in Jerry's hand work — the word "Scrabble" in the caption does the identification.
Fallback: Remove the Scrabble board. Jerry at a plain table, holding something small in his hand. The caption identifies Scrabble; the visual just needs Jerry's expression.
Jerry hook: "Jerry still holding the tiles after his wife has quit the game forever is the character detail that makes this concept work. He didn't cheat and feel bad about it. He cheated and kept the evidence. The abandoned board behind him is the crime scene and he's still at it."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 8.8 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 4: punchline + tiles visible in hand + abandoned board, Scene 2: setup with "cheating" delivery] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.2s]

---

**Concept B — "The Evidence"**
Tighter concept — Jerry opens his hand to reveal the Scrabble tiles at the punchline moment. The tiles are hidden during the setup (fist closed or hand at his side). The visual reveal mirrors the narrative reveal.
Duration: 8.4s — The hand-open reveal is the visual punchline; tighter runtime keeps the surprise sharp.

Master video prompt:
"Plain warm background or kitchen counter area, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm lighting, vertical 9:16 portrait. Jerry Gold (expressive eyebrows, handlebar mustache, casual shirt) facing camera. One hand at his side or held in a loose fist — concealing Scrabble tiles. At the punchline, the hand opens palm-up: several Scrabble letter tiles revealed — small wooden squares with black printed letters, clearly legible. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–1.8s): HOOK
A simple warm background — a kitchen counter edge, or a plain wall with warm light. Jerry Gold faces the viewer — expressive eyebrows, handlebar mustache, casual shirt. His expression is mild sheepishness — the beginning of a confession. One hand hangs at his side in a loose fist. Something small is inside the fist — the shape of small objects visible in his grip, but not identifiable. His other hand is relaxed. He has something to say and something to show, and he's going to do them in the correct order.

Scene 2 (1.8–4.8s): SETUP
Same background. Jerry Gold (mustache, casual shirt) faces the viewer, sheepish. His closed fist remains at his side or at waist height — whatever is inside is not yet revealed. He delivers the setup with the measured tone of a confession. "Cheating" is in the sentence and he knows the weight of it. "Letters" is in the sentence and the audience assigns it one meaning. His closed fist holds the truth. The audience cannot see it yet. His expression is a man walking into a conversation he knows will go badly and proceeding anyway.
Caption: "My wife found out I was cheating on her after she found all the letters I was hiding."

Scene 3 (4.8–5.2s): BEAT
Same background. Jerry pauses. His closed fist moves slightly — lifting toward the viewer. His expression shifts from sheepish to incredulous. He is about to show you something.

Scene 4 (5.2–8.4s): PUNCHLINE — THE REVEAL
Same background. Jerry Gold (mustache, casual shirt) opens his hand — palm up, facing the viewer. In his open palm: several Scrabble letter tiles. Small wooden squares, each printed with a black letter — S, A, E, R, and a couple more, clearly legible as game pieces. These are the letters. These are what he was hiding. They are tiny wooden squares from a board game. His expression above the open hand is mild incredulity — the face of a man presenting evidence that should exonerate him but apparently does not. He was cheating at Scrabble. He was hiding Scrabble tiles. His wife found the Scrabble tiles and declared the game over. He is still unclear on whether this response was proportional. The tiles sit in his open palm, innocent, incriminating, and worth approximately 12 points.
Caption: "She said she's never playing Scrabble with me again."

Caption plan: 0.0–1.8s silent | 1.8–4.8s setup | 5.2–8.4s punchline.
Voice: Same Jerry delivery. 0.4s pause. Flat punchline with reported speech.
Risk: LOW. The hand-open reveal needs the tiles to be visible and recognizable as Scrabble tiles — small wooden squares with letters. AI gen may render them as generic objects. Fallback: Skip the hand reveal. Jerry holds a Scrabble tile bag (cloth drawstring pouch) and shakes it at the camera — the prop says "Scrabble" without requiring tiny tile details.
Jerry hook: "The closed-fist-to-open-palm reveal makes the audience physically lean forward — they want to see what he was hiding. The reveal is tiny wooden Scrabble tiles. The anti-climax of the physical reveal mirrors the anti-climax of the narrative reveal. He was hiding game pieces. That's it."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 8.4 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 4: open-palm tile reveal + Jerry's incredulity, Scene 2: closed-fist setup + sheepish delivery] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.2s]

---

quality_gate: PASS — Two meaningfully different concepts (Scrabble-board crime scene vs. closed-fist evidence reveal), full timestamps, punchline readable, vertical framing preserved, fallbacks provided.
status: ready_for_jerry_review
